title: AI agent instructions: best practices, examples, and template
description: Learn how to write effective AI agent instructions in DronaHQ so your agent knows its role, tone, boundaries, tool behavior, knowledge usage, and how to respond reliably across real workflows.
keywords: AI agent instructions, write agent instructions, DronaHQ instruction guide, agent behavior design, instruction template

mainEntity.name: What should AI agent instructions include?

acceptedAnswer.text: AI agent instructions should define the agent's role, task boundaries, tone, expected behavior, tool-use rules, knowledge access, response format, and how to handle ambiguity, low confidence, or escalation. A good instruction set helps the agent act consistently across real workflows instead of responding like a generic chatbot.

mainEntity.name: How are agent instructions different from prompts?

acceptedAnswer.text: Agent instructions define the agent's ongoing behavior, rules, and operating context. Prompts are the task-specific inputs a user sends during a conversation. In simple terms, instructions shape how the agent should behave every time, while prompts tell it what to work on in a given moment.

mainEntity.name: How long should agent instructions be?

acceptedAnswer.text: Agent instructions should be as detailed as needed to make the agent reliable, but not so long that they become repetitive, conflicting, or hard to follow. The goal is clarity, structure, and coverage of important behaviors rather than length for its own sake.

mainEntity.name: What are common mistakes in writing agent instructions?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common mistakes include giving the agent a vague role, mixing too many rules into one block, leaving tool usage undefined, skipping edge cases, not specifying boundaries, and failing to explain how the agent should respond when it is unsure. Weak instructions often make the agent sound generic or behave inconsistently.

mainEntity.name: Should agent instructions include tool-use rules?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. If an agent can use tools, the instructions should explain when to use them, what each tool is for, and any limits or precautions around tool execution. This helps the agent know when to fetch data, take action, ask for confirmation, or avoid unnecessary tool calls.

mainEntity.name: How do you handle uncertainty in agent instructions?

acceptedAnswer.text: Agent instructions should tell the agent what to do when the input is unclear, the answer is uncertain, or the request falls outside its scope. This often includes asking clarifying questions, avoiding unsupported claims, escalating when needed, or clearly stating that more information is required.

step.text: Add instructions for ambiguity, low confidence, unsupported requests, and escalation paths.

step.text: Review the agent behavior in practice and improve the instructions based on observed responses.

title: Introduction to building AI agents on DronaHQ
description: Get started with DronaHQ Agents and learn how to build AI agents that reason over context, use tools, respond through chat or business channels, and run real workflows across your systems.
keywords: DronaHQ Agents, AI agents platform, build AI agents, agentic workflows, business automation agents

mainEntity.name: What are DronaHQ Agents?

acceptedAnswer.text: DronaHQ Agents are AI agents that can reason over context, use tools, retrieve knowledge, and execute real workflows across business systems. They are designed to do more than answer questions by helping teams automate tasks, update systems, and handle operational work.

mainEntity.name: How are DronaHQ Agents different from a basic chatbot or agent framework?

acceptedAnswer.text: DronaHQ Agents are built for execution, not just conversation. Along with model reasoning, they include structured interfaces, triggers, tools, knowledge base, guardrails, and traces so agents can operate safely across apps, databases, APIs, and external business systems.

mainEntity.name: What do I need to get started with DronaHQ Agents?

acceptedAnswer.text: To get started, you need a DronaHQ account and access to DronaHQ Agents. From there, you can create an agent, add instructions, connect tools or knowledge sources, choose where the agent should run, and begin testing it.

mainEntity.name: What core components can I configure in a DronaHQ Agent?

acceptedAnswer.text: A DronaHQ Agent can be configured with instructions, tools, triggers, knowledge base, and safety controls. Together, these help define what the agent should do, what data or systems it can access, how it gets activated, and how it stays reliable in production.

mainEntity.name: Where can DronaHQ Agents run?

acceptedAnswer.text: DronaHQ Agents can run inside an embedded chat interface as well as through external channels and triggers such as WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook Messenger, email, webhooks, scheduled runs, and connected business events.

mainEntity.name: Can DronaHQ Agents work with company data and business tools?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. DronaHQ Agents can connect to internal databases, APIs, enterprise tools, and knowledge sources so they can retrieve information, take actions, and complete tasks using approved business context.

mainEntity.name: How do DronaHQ Agents stay grounded and safe?

acceptedAnswer.text: DronaHQ Agents can use a knowledge base to ground responses in approved sources, tools to work with live systems, and guardrails to control what goes into and comes out of the model. Traces also help teams inspect how the agent reasoned, what tools it used, and how each run performed.

mainEntity.name: What kinds of workflows can I build with DronaHQ Agents?

acceptedAnswer.text: You can build agents for use cases such as lead qualification, customer support, reconciliation, appointment scheduling, internal knowledge assistance, IT service tasks, and other workflows that need both reasoning and action across connected systems.

acceptedAnswer.text: Guardrails are safety and policy controls that manage how inputs and outputs are handled so the agent behaves in a safe, compliant, and reliable way.

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Guardrails can apply to both incoming prompts and outgoing responses, depending on the configured policies.

acceptedAnswer.text: A protected app is an app that requires authentication or access controls before users can open and use it.

mainEntity.name: Why use a protected app instead of a public app?

acceptedAnswer.text: Protected apps are better for internal tools, sensitive workflows, and business data because they limit access to the right users and reduce exposure.

mainEntity.name: Why track usage for public apps and embeds separately?

acceptedAnswer.text: Public and embedded experiences may have different traffic patterns and sharing behavior, so separate tracking helps teams plan exposure, cost, and scale more clearly.

mainEntity.name: What is this action block used for?

acceptedAnswer.text: This action block is used to perform a specific step inside an app workflow.

mainEntity.name: When should you use an action block in a workflow?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use an action block when your workflow needs to trigger an action, control app behavior, or handle logic during runtime.

mainEntity.name: Why use ready action blocks instead of building workflow logic manually?

acceptedAnswer.text: Ready action blocks help build workflows faster, keep logic more consistent, and reduce manual implementation effort.

title: Connect to Airtable | Ready integration

mainEntity.name: What is this connector used for?

acceptedAnswer.text: This connector makes it very easy to configure the integration in DronaHQ. It can be used to connect external systems, services, or data sources with apps and workflows in DronaHQ.

mainEntity.name: Why should you use a connector in an app or workflow?

acceptedAnswer.text: A connector helps apps and workflows read data, send updates, and trigger actions without building every integration from scratch.

mainEntity.name: What can you do with a connector?

acceptedAnswer.text: A connector can be used to fetch records, push data, trigger external actions, and support integration workflows across business systems.

howto_schema.name: Set up and use a connector
howto_schema.description: Step-by-step guide to connect an external service or data source to apps and workflows

step.name: Choose the connector
step.text: Select the connector that matches the external system, service, or data source you want to use from a vast library of pre-built connectors.

step.name: Configure authentication
step.text: Enter the credentials, tokens, or connection details required for secure access.

step.name: Create the query or action
step.text: Define the data operation or external action the app or workflow should run.

step.name: Test the integration
step.text: Run the connector and confirm that the expected data or action works correctly.

step.text: Select the connector that matches the external system, service, or data source you want to use from a vast library of pre-built connectors.

step.name: Configure authentication
step.text: Enter the credentials, tokens, or connection details required for secure access.

step.name: Create the query or action
step.text: Define the data operation or external action the app or workflow should run.

step.name: Test the integration
step.text: Run the connector and confirm that the expected data or action works correctly.

title: Connect to HubSpot | Ready integration
description: Integrate HubSpot to your apps at data source using ready connector in DronaHQ
keywords: integrate HubSpot, HubSpot connector

mainEntity.name: What is this connector used for?

acceptedAnswer.text: This connector makes it very easy to configure the integration in DronaHQ. It can be used to connect external systems, services, or data sources with apps and workflows in DronaHQ.

mainEntity.name: Why should you use a connector in an app or workflow?

acceptedAnswer.text: A connector helps apps and workflows read data, send updates, and trigger actions without building every integration from scratch.

mainEntity.name: What can you do with a connector?

acceptedAnswer.text: A connector can be used to fetch records, push data, trigger external actions, and support integration workflows across business systems.

acceptedAnswer.text: A connector helps apps and workflows read data, send updates, and trigger actions without building every integration from scratch.

title: Query language function reference for data retrieval and transformation
description: Explore the DronaHQ Query Language function reference to find built-in functions for retrieving values, transforming data, building expressions, and shaping query output.
keywords: query language function reference, DQL functions, data transformation functions, query expressions, function reference for data queries

mainEntity.name: What is a query language function reference used for?

acceptedAnswer.text: A function reference helps developers find the built-in expressions available for filtering, transforming, and returning data in query workflows.

mainEntity.name: Why are built-in query functions useful in app development?

acceptedAnswer.text: Built-in query functions help shape data, reduce repetitive logic, and make query behavior more precise without requiring full custom scripts for every use case.

mainEntity.name: What kinds of tasks can query language functions help with?

acceptedAnswer.text: They can help with value extraction, data manipulation, conditional selection, and returning structured output for apps and workflows.

howto_schema.name: Use the query language function reference
